sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a 3-month history of intermittent cough, which began approximately 3 months ago while working with horses, and has been exacerbated by a recent cold.
The patient reports that the cough has improved with exercise, specifically biking.
The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, currently managed with methotrexate 7.5mg weekly, and has been a former smoker for approximately 7 years.
The patient has a family history of breast cancer in their mother, but no history of lung cancer or other malignancies.
The patient denies any previous history of lung infections or pneumonia.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate 7.5mg weekly.
The patient is a former smoker, having stopped approximately 7 years ago.
The patient's mother has a history of breast cancer.
Review of systems: The patient denies any pain, shortness of breath, or other symptoms.
Current medications: I prescribed methotrexate 7.5mg weekly for rheumatoid arthritis.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure is 124/76, heart rate is 70, respiration rate is 16, and oxygen saturation on room air is 98%.
The patient is afebrile.
Physical exam: The patient's lungs are clear and equal bilateral, with no abnormalities noted on auscultation.
The heart has a regular rate and rhythm, with no clicks, rubs, or murmurs.
The patient has mild swelling to the knuckles on both hands, which is consistent with rheumatoid arthritis.
Pulses are intact in all extremities, and capillary refill is brisk.
Test Results: The patient had a chest CT scan prior to the visit, which showed a solitary 2cm nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe.
The nodule is smooth in appearance, and there is no evidence of emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule, which requires further evaluation.
Problem list: Lung nodule; Rheumatoid arthritis; Former smoker; Family history of breast cancer.
Plan: I will order a pulmonary function test (PFT) to assess lung function.
I will schedule a PET CT to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.
I will recommend a video-assisted thoracoscopy to remove the nodule, which will involve a general anesthesia and a hospital stay of one night.
The patient will have a chest tube in place until the following day.
The patient will continue to follow up with their rheumatologist and maintain their current medication therapy for rheumatoid arthritis.
Follow-up: The patient will be scheduled for a PET CT scan and a video-assisted thoracoscopy to remove the lung nodule.
